ANALYSI S
Section 87100 prohibits a public official 
from naking,
participating in uraking, or using his or her position to influence
i qovernmental decision in which the official 
knows or has reason
to know he or she has a f i-nanciaL interest. 
An official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a naterial financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
officiit 
or a nember of his or her inmediate farnily or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or Dore.
(b) Any real property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or nore.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a comnercial ).ending
institution in the regular course of business on

Page 4
terrns available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating trro hundred fifty
dolfars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the tirne r/hen the
decision i.s made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of nanagernent.
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dotlars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
section 87103 (a) - (e) .
Mayor Hughes and counciLnembers Maxfield and Beck are members
of the catistoga city council; therefore, they are public
officials 
under the Act. 
(Section a2o4a. ) Under the foregoing
sections, the mayor and city councilmembers nay not particiPate in
any decision which will have a reasonabLy foreseeabLe material
fiiancial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generall,y, on any of the econonic interests described above.
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the tine a governmental decision is made
aepenas on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
co-nsidered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it rri1l occur. certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a nere possibility, 
it is not
reasonabl-y foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC oPs. 198,
copy enclosed.) clearly, changing zoning and land use
delignations in the city is going to have some financial effect on
the mayor's and councilnembers' economic interests. 
For exaDp1e,
you informed me on the telephone that the updated general plan
presently recommends that Councilmember lilaxf ield's undeveLoped
silverado property be a planned development.
rn addition, the foreseeable effect on the mayor's and
counci lrnembers' economic interests also must be naterial to
require disqualificatj.on. 
The conmission has adopted regulations
which provide guidance on whether the reasonably foreseeable
effects of a decision will- be considered rnaterial. 
RegTulation
18702 (copy enclosed) sets forth the guidelines for deterniningr
whether an official's 
economi.c interest in a decision is
I'rnateria11y" affected as required by Section 87103. If the
official's 
economic interest is directLv involved in the decision,
then Regulation 18702.1 (copy enclosed) applies to determine
rnateriality. 
ReguLation 18702.1(b) describes when a subject is
deemed to be directly invotved in a decision. on the other hand,


Fi■e No. ■-90-542
Page 5
if the official's 
economic interest is indirectlv affected by the
decision, then Regrulations 18702.2 to 7a702.5 (copies enclosed)
uould aDDIv to deternine whether the effect of the decision is
rnateriai.'7
Particination in a Governmental Decision
you also asked whether it was permissible for Mayor llughes
and councilmembers uaxfield and Beck to participate in infornal
public workshops concerning the general plan and the environmental
inpact report. 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official 
fron
makinq, participatino in nakincr, or in anv wav attemolinq t9 use
his ofiilial 
position to j-nfluence a governmental decision in
wffi 
fre knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.
This prohibition is interpreted broadly in order to fulfill 
the
purpoLes of the Act which are best served by a rule which-
rnininizes participation in a governnental decision by officials
with a conflict of interest. 
(fn re Hudson (1978) 4 FPPC ops. 13,
copy enclosed. )
An official 
is pefli.q.lpeling in a decision when he or she
does any of the foJ,lowing:
(1) Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity or
private person regarding the decision; or
(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-naker, either directly or rrithout
significant intervening substantive review, by:
(A) Conducting research or rnaking any
investigation which requires the exercise of
j udgrrnent on the part of the officiaL or
designated employee and the purPose of which
is to influence the decisionr' or
(B) Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, oraIly or in writing,
which reguires the exercise of j udgrment on the
part of the official 
or designated enployee
4/ 
(see the following advice letters for a conf l ict-of-interest
analysis of city officials 
and their participation in decisions
concerninq the general plan: llarino Advice Letter, No. I-89-291i
waqqoner Advice Letter, No. f-89-284; and casev Advice Letter, No.
n-e?-0a8; copies enclosed.) You will observe that the rnateriality

irom the guidelines applied today. That is because the

However, the analysis merely requires the application of the

and the purpose of irhich is to influence the

An of f ici.al is atteropting to use his or her official 
position
to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing the

contacts, appears before, or otherwise

consequently, ttre councilmembers

official 
may appear in the sane nanner as any other member of the

From the facts you have provided, it appears that in certain

and the mayor still

councilmenber Maxf ield
Based on the information you have provided, councilmenber
Maxfield has two types of the econonic interests described in
section 87103 in the jurisdiction of the city of Calistoga: he
has real property interests worth $1,000 or nore, and he receives

The test for determining whether the effects of a decision on
an interest in real property are uaterial depends on whether the
interest in real property is directly or indirectly involved in
the decision. Regulation la102.1(a) (3) describes situations in
which real property interests are considered materially affected
because of the direct irnpact of the decision on the real property,
These incLude the following situations:
(A) The decision j.nvolves the zoning or
rezoning, annexation or reannexation, sa1e,
purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or excLusion
from any city, county, district 
or other local
governnental subdivision, of real property in
lrhich the official. has a direct or indirect
interest (other than a leasehold interest) of
$1,000 or nore, or a similar decision affecting
such property r'
(B) The decision involves the j,ssuance,
denial or revocation of a license, pernit or
other land use entitlement authorizing a specific
use or uses of such property;
(C) The decision involves the inposition,
repeal or nodification of any taxes or fees
assessed or iurposed on such property; or

area, to select the project area, to adoPt the

to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redeveLopBent plan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area, or to rescind or anend any of
the above decisions; and real property in which the
official 
has an interest, or any part of it is
Located within the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment area.
(E) For purposes of this suMivision, the
terns t'zonj,ngrt and trrezoning'i shaIl refer to the
act of establishing or changing the zoning or land
use designation on the subject property, but sha11
not refer to an amendment of an existing zoning
ordinance or other land use regulation (such as
changes in the uses permitted, or developrnent
standards applicable, within a particular zoningf
category) which is applicable to all other
properties desigrnated in that category.

decisions herein will have a direct effect on councilmember
Maxfield's real property interests.

nore; or

interests in substantially the
same manner as it rrif I affect a significant segment of the public

Because adoption of the updated general plan usually involves

However, once a1I decisions related to the updated general plan

that they nay participate in decisions concerning geographical
areas covered by the report and plan other than in which their
financial interests are located. we have advised that large,
complex decisions under certain circumstances rnay be divided into
separate decisions hrhen an official 
has a disqualifying interest
in one component of the decision which is not interdependent upon
other conponents. The official 
rnay then participate as to the
other components in which he has no financial interest. 
(see,
Killian Advice Letter, No. A-89-522 and Huffaker Advice Letter,
No. A-86-343, copies enclosed.) However, under some
circumstances, a series of decisions may be too interrelated to be
considered separately. (Miller Advice Letter, No. A-82-119,
supra. )
Because of the possible interrelationship of land use
designation and zoning areas, the nayor and councilmembers may
participate in decisions regarding areas where they have no
economic interests only if the decision on any one component will
not have a reasonably foreseeable naterial financial effect upon
their interests. 
(Iluffaker Advice Letter, supra.) If the
decision will not have such an effect, they nay participate if the
procedure outlined below is adhered to by the city council:
(1) The decisions from which the nayor and the
counci.lrnembers have a disqualifying financial interest are
segregated from the other decisions.
(2) The decisions from which the mayor and councilnerbers
are disqualified are considered first, 
and a final decision is
reached by the city council without the mayor and councilmembers
participating in any way.
(3) once a final decision has been nade on that geographical
area, the nayor and the councilmembers nay participate in the
deliberations and vote regarding the other areas within the
general plan, so long as those deliberations and vote do not
result in a reopening or in any way affect the decisions fron
which they were disqualified.

sections.question:

Because of the possible interrelationship of land use
designation and zoning areas, the nayor and councilmembers may
participate in decisions regarding areas where they have no
economic interests only if the decision on any one component will
not have a reasonably foreseeable naterial financial effect upon
their interests. 
(Iluffaker Advice Letter, supra.) If the
decision will not have such an effect, they nay participate if the
procedure outlined below is adhered to by the city council:
(1) The decisions from which the nayor and the
counci.lrnembers have a disqualifying financial interest are
segregated from the other decisions.
(2) The decisions from which the mayor and councilnerbers
are disqualified are considered first, 
and a final decision is
reached by the city council without the mayor and councilmembers
participating in any way.
(3) once a final decision has been nade on that geographical
area, the nayor and the councilmembers nay participate in the
deliberations and vote regarding the other areas within the
general plan, so long as those deliberations and vote do not
result in a reopening or in any way affect the decisions fron
which they were disqualified.
